payload.description: Chrome extension that automatically applies a dark theme to sites that lack one. Approach: deterministic local CSS transformation, no LLM and no network calls. v1 engine is a smart inversion filter (invert + hue-rotate on the root, counter-invert media) injected at document_start, with already-dark detection to skip sites that have their own night mode, and per-site settings via a popup. Vanilla JS, Manifest V3, no build step, no external dependencies.
payload.title: Serenity: auto night mode Chrome extension (MV3)

payload.description:
Create the extension skeleton.

Files:
- manifest.json (Manifest V3): name Serenity, version 0.1.0, permissions: [storage, tabs], action popup, content script declared statically: matches <all_urls>, run_at document_start, all_frames FALSE (top frame only - the root filter also inverts iframe pixels, so per-frame injection would double-invert).
- src/content.js - content script entry (engine wired in later task; for now a no-op IIFE that reads settings).
- src/popup.html, src/popup.js, src/popup.css - popup shell.
- src/settings.js - storage schema + helpers, loaded by popup; content script duplicates the tiny read helper inline or via a second declared content-script file (content scripts cannot use ES module imports; declare multiple files in manifest content_scripts js array, they share scope).
- README.md - what it is, how to load unpacked (chrome://extensions, Developer mode, Load unpacked), file layout.
- Simple icon set (16/48/128 png) - a plain dark circle/moon generated however is convenient; placeholder quality is fine.

Storage schema (chrome.storage.sync, fall back to local if sync unavailable):
{ globalEnabled: true, defaults: { brightness: 100, contrast: 100 }, sites: { [origin]: { mode: 'auto'|'on'|'off', brightness?, contrast? } } }
Provide helpers: getSettings(), getSiteConfig(origin), setSiteConfig(origin, patch).

Constraints: vanilla JS only, no build step, no external dependencies, no frameworks.
payload.title: Scaffold MV3 extension

payload.description:
Implement the core dark mode engine in src/content.js.

At document_start (before <head> exists), synchronously read cached settings and, unless site mode is 'off' or globalEnabled is false, append a <style id=serenity-style> element to document.documentElement with:

html { filter: invert(1) hue-rotate(180deg) brightness(Bx) contrast(Cx) !important; background: #fff !important; }
img, picture, video, canvas, embed, object { filter: invert(1) hue-rotate(180deg) !important; }
:root { color-scheme: dark !important; }

Notes and gotchas (encode these in code comments only where non-obvious):
- html background is forced to #fff so pages relying on the default white paint invert to dark instead of staying transparent.
- Do NOT counter-invert iframe elements: the root filter inverts iframe pixels, which is the desired effect for frames; counter-inverting would render frame content light. all_frames stays false.
- Brightness/contrast come from per-site config falling back to defaults; values are percents (100 = neutral), composed into the root filter.
- chrome.storage is async, but injection must be flash-free. Strategy: inject dark style immediately with last-known settings mirrored in a synchronous cache. Simplest reliable v1: always inject immediately with neutral defaults, then chrome.storage.sync.get resolves within a few ms and either removes the style (disabled) or updates the filter values. The brief dark flash on disabled sites is acceptable for v1; a white flash on enabled sites is not.
- Expose two functions used by later tasks: applyTheme(config) (creates or updates the style element) and removeTheme().
- Listen for chrome.storage.onChanged and for runtime messages ({type:'serenity:update'}) to re-apply or remove the theme live without reload.
- SVG icons inline in the page invert with the page - that is fine and usually desirable; do not counter-invert svg.

Manual sanity check: load extension, visit a light site (e.g. wikipedia.org) - page renders dark with no white flash, photos look normal, form controls dark.
payload.title: Filter-based dark engine in content script

payload.description:
Skip sites that already ship a dark theme.

In content.js, on DOMContentLoaded (and re-check on window load):
- Compute the page's effective background color IGNORING our own filter: read getComputedStyle backgroundColor of body then html; treat fully transparent as white (default paint). Note: CSS filters do not change computed backgroundColor values, so reading computed style is safe even while our style is applied.
- Convert to relative luminance (standard WCAG formula, plain function). If luminance < 0.35, the site is natively dark.
- If natively dark and the site mode is 'auto': call removeTheme() and record the detection in storage under sites[origin].detectedDark = { value: true, ts: <epoch ms> } so the popup can display 'skipped - already dark' and so the NEXT page load can skip injection entirely (no dark flash). Cache expires after 7 days (re-detect).
- Mode 'on' overrides detection (theme stays even on dark sites); mode 'off' never injects.
- Also treat <meta name=color-scheme content~=dark> combined with matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)').matches as a native-dark signal.
- Keep the luminance helper a small pure function.

Manual sanity check: github.com in dark appearance or news.ycombinator.com with a dark userstyle stays untouched; second visit to a detected-dark site has no dark flash at all.
payload.title: Already-dark site detection and auto-skip

payload.description:
Build the popup (src/popup.html/js/css).

Contents, top to bottom:
- Global on/off toggle.
- Current site line: origin + status text (On - auto / Skipped - site is already dark / Forced on / Off).
- Tri-state site control: Auto | Always on | Off (radio group or segmented control) writing sites[origin].mode.
- Brightness and Contrast sliders (range 50-150, default 100) writing per-site values; a small reset button restores defaults.
- Footer note with version.

Behavior:
- On open, read the active tab (chrome.tabs.query) to get origin; handle non-http(s) pages (chrome://, file://, web store) by disabling site controls with an explanatory line.
- Every change persists via settings helpers, then chrome.tabs.sendMessage(tabId, {type:'serenity:update'}) so the content script re-applies instantly. Handle the sendMessage rejection when no content script is present (e.g. tab predates install) by ignoring it.
- Popup itself is dark-themed, compact (~280px wide), plain CSS, no frameworks.

Manual sanity check: toggling site off removes dark instantly without reload; sliders visibly change the page live; settings survive browser restart.
payload.title: Popup UI with per-site controls

payload.description:
Add test/fixtures/*.html - small self-contained pages exercising the edge cases:
- light.html: plain light article page (baseline).
- dark.html: natively dark page (background #111) - must be auto-skipped.
- media.html: photos (img), an HTML5 video element, a canvas drawing, inline SVG icons.
- fixed.html: position:fixed header + sticky sidebar over a long scrolling body (filter containing-block edge case).
- frame.html: page embedding light.html and dark.html in iframes.
Serve with python3 -m http.server from repo root (document in TESTING.md; note detection is per-origin so fixtures share one origin - use the site tri-state to reset between checks).

Write TESTING.md: load-unpacked steps, the fixture checks above with expected results, plus a real-site pass list: wikipedia.org, news.ycombinator.com, developer.mozilla.org, github.com, a Google search results page. For each: no white flash on load, photos/video look natural, fixed headers behave while scrolling, already-dark sites untouched, popup controls work live, settings persist.

No automated browser test harness for v1 - keep it small. If straightforward, add a tiny node script or plain assertions page for the luminance pure function; skip if it adds any dependency.
payload.title: Test fixtures and QA checklist

payload.description: Future work, NOT in v1 scope. Replace/augment the inversion filter with true dynamic theming: iterate document.styleSheets, rewrite color/background/border values deterministically (flip lightness, preserve hue), inject overrides, handle inline styles and mutations via MutationObserver, fetch cross-origin stylesheets through the background service worker. Higher fidelity than inversion (real dark palette, images untouched, no filter containing-block quirks) at the cost of substantially more code. Only pick this up after v1 proves insufficient on sites that matter.
payload.title: v2: dynamic palette engine (Dark Reader-style)

Add test/fixtures/*.html - small self-contained pages exercising the edge cases:
- light.html: plain light article page (baseline).
- dark.html: natively dark page (background #111) - must be auto-skipped.
- media.html: photos (img), an HTML5 video element, a canvas drawing, inline SVG icons.
- fixed.html: position:fixed header + sticky sidebar over a long scrolling body (filter containing-block edge case).

Write TESTING.md: load-unpacked steps, the fixture checks above with expected results, plus a real-site pass list: wikipedia.org, news.ycombinator.com, developer.mozilla.org, github.com, a Google search results page. For each: no white flash on load, photos/video look natural, fixed headers behave while scrolling, already-dark sites untouched, popup controls work live, settings persist.

No automated browser test harness for v1 - keep it small. If straightforward, add a tiny node script or plain assertions page for the luminance pure function; skip if it adds any dependency.

Chrome extension that automatically applies a dark theme to sites that lack one. Approach: deterministic local CSS transformation, no LLM and no network calls. v1 engine is a smart inversion filter (invert + hue-rotate on the root, counter-invert media) injected at document_start, with already-dark detection to skip sites that have their own night mode, and per-site settings via a popup. Vanilla JS, Manifest V3, no build step, no external dependencies.

Three findings from review of the v1 implementation. 1. DETECTION BLIND SPOT (bug): the engine injects html { background: #fff !important }, and effectiveBackgroundColor() falls back to reading html's computed backgroundColor. A natively dark site that sets its dark background only on html (body transparent) reads as WHITE because our own rule wins, so it is never detected as dark and gets inverted to light. Fix: in isNativelyDark()/effectiveBackgroundColor(), temporarily set the injected style element's .disabled = true, measure computed colors, then restore .disabled. This is synchronous so nothing paints in between. Also change test/fixtures/dark.html to set the dark background on html (not body, body transparent) so the fixture would catch this regression. 2. PICTURE DOUBLE-FILTER (bug): the counter-invert selector includes both picture and img. For markup (common on Wikipedia and news sites), the img filter and the picture filter cancel each other, leaving only the root inversion, so photos render inverted. Fix: remove picture from the selector - the img inside is the element that paints. Add a with + to test/fixtures/media.html so the fixture covers it. 3. DETECTION CACHE POLLUTES SYNC STORAGE (robustness): recordDetectedDark() writes sites[origin].detectedDark into chrome.storage.sync for every dark site visited. The whole sites map is one sync item with an 8KB quota, so this overflows after ~100 origins, silently breaks writes (no chrome.runtime.lastError check anywhere), and syncs a list of visited origins across devices. Fix: keep user-set overrides (mode/brightness/contrast) in sync, but store detections in chrome.storage.local under a separate top-level key: detections = { [origin]: ts }. Content script and popup read both (same 7-day TTL logic). Check chrome.runtime.lastError in storage set callbacks and drop the write silently if set fails. One commit per finding or one combined commit is fine; close this issue when all three are done.
payload.title: Review fixes round 1: detection blind spot, picture double-filter, detection cache in sync

payload.description: Three findings from review of the v1 implementation. 1. DETECTION BLIND SPOT (bug): the engine injects html { background: #fff !important }, and effectiveBackgroundColor() falls back to reading html's computed backgroundColor. A natively dark site that sets its dark background only on html (body transparent) reads as WHITE because our own rule wins, so it is never detected as dark and gets inverted to light. Fix: in isNativelyDark()/effectiveBackgroundColor(), temporarily set the injected style element's .disabled = true, measure computed colors, then restore .disabled. This is synchronous so nothing paints in between. Also change test/fixtures/dark.html to set the dark background on html (not body, body transparent) so the fixture would catch this regression. 2. PICTURE DOUBLE-FILTER (bug): the counter-invert selector includes both picture and img. For markup (common on Wikipedia and news sites), the img filter and the picture filter cancel each other, leaving only the root inversion, so photos render inverted. Fix: remove picture from the selector - the img inside is the element that paints. Add a with + to test/fixtures/media.html so the fixture covers it. 3. DETECTION CACHE POLLUTES SYNC STORAGE (robustness): recordDetectedDark() writes sites[origin].detectedDark into chrome.storage.sync for every dark site visited. The whole sites map is one sync item with an 8KB quota, so this overflows after ~100 origins, silently breaks writes (no chrome.runtime.lastError check anywhere), and syncs a list of visited origins across devices. Fix: keep user-set overrides (mode/brightness/contrast) in sync, but store detections in chrome.storage.local under a separate top-level key: detections = { [origin]: ts }. Content script and popup read both (same 7-day TTL logic). Check chrome.runtime.lastError in storage set callbacks and drop the write silently if set fails. One commit per finding or one combined commit is fine; close this issue when all three are done.
payload.title: Review fixes round 1: detection blind spot, picture double-filter, detection cache in sync

payload.description: Three findings from review of the v1 implementation. 1. DETECTION BLIND SPOT (bug): the engine injects html { background: #fff !important }, and effectiveBackgroundColor() falls back to reading html's computed backgroundColor. A natively dark site that sets its dark background only on html (body transparent) reads as WHITE because our own rule wins, so it is never detected as dark and gets inverted to light. Fix: in isNativelyDark()/effectiveBackgroundColor(), temporarily set the injected style element's .disabled = true, measure computed colors, then restore .disabled. This is synchronous so nothing paints in between. Also change test/fixtures/dark.html to set the dark background on html (not body, body transparent) so the fixture would catch this regression. 2. PICTURE DOUBLE-FILTER (bug): the counter-invert selector includes both picture and img. For markup (common on Wikipedia and news sites), the img filter and the picture filter cancel each other, leaving only the root inversion, so photos render inverted. Fix: remove picture from the selector - the img inside is the element that paints. Add a with + to test/fixtures/media.html so the fixture covers it. 3. DETECTION CACHE POLLUTES SYNC STORAGE (robustness): recordDetectedDark() writes sites[origin].detectedDark into chrome.storage.sync for every dark site visited. The whole sites map is one sync item with an 8KB quota, so this overflows after ~100 origins, silently breaks writes (no chrome.runtime.lastError check anywhere), and syncs a list of visited origins across devices. Fix: keep user-set overrides (mode/brightness/contrast) in sync, but store detections in chrome.storage.local under a separate top-level key: detections = { [origin]: ts }. Content script and popup read both (same 7-day TTL logic). Check chrome.runtime.lastError in storage set callbacks and drop the write silently if set fails. One commit per finding or one combined commit is fine; close this issue when all three are done.

Found during live QA: getComputedStyle backgroundColor returns modern color serializations that parseRgb() cannot read, so effectiveBackgroundColor() falls back to 'white' and misjudges the page. Real example from substack.com (their whole design system uses wide-gamut colors): body backgroundColor computes to color(display-p3 0.086 0.09 0.094) - a nearly-black background that we fail to parse, treat as white, and therefore invert a dark page to light (or mis-skip a light one). Likely the cause of the reported Substack dashboard misbehavior.

Fixes, all in src/content.js:

1. Replace parseRgb() with a parseColor() that handles:
   - rgb(r, g, b) / rgba(r, g, b, a) comma syntax (current behavior, 0-255 ints)
   - rgb(r g b / a) modern space-slash syntax (0-255, alpha optional)
   - color(srgb r g b [/ a]) and color(display-p3 r g b [/ a]) with 0.0-1.0 floats, scaled to 0-255. Treat display-p3 channels as if sRGB - the approximation error is irrelevant for a dark/light threshold at 0.35 luminance.
   Return null for anything else (lab, lch, oklch etc.) exactly as before - null falls through to the next probe / white fallback.

2. Tighten hasDarkColorSchemeMeta(): only treat the meta as a dark signal when its content is EXACTLY 'dark' (trimmed, case-insensitive, possibly 'only dark'). content='light dark' merely declares the site supports both schemes and must NOT count - a site declaring 'light dark' that actually renders dark will still be caught by the luminance probe, while a light-rendering one was being falsely skipped when the OS is in dark mode.

3. Fixtures: change test/fixtures/dark.html html background to background: color(display-p3 0.086 0.09 0.094) (the real Substack value) so the p3 path is covered by the existing auto-skip check. Add a <meta name='color-scheme' content='light dark'> to test/fixtures/light.html - it must still get themed (meta must not cause a skip).

4. Keep relativeLuminance() unchanged; it takes 0-255 channels.

Run node --check on touched files and re-validate manifest references before committing. One commit, close this issue when done. Do not touch other open issues.
payload.title: Detection: parse modern CSS color syntaxes (display-p3, space-separated rgb)

payload.description: UX gap found after fixing detection bugs: a stale false-positive detection (detections[origin] in chrome.storage.local) keeps suppressing the theme for up to 7 days even after the detector itself is fixed or the page changes. Fix: (1) whenever the user changes the site tri-state in the popup, delete detections[origin] so auto mode re-evaluates on next load; (2) optionally add a small 'Re-check site' link in the popup that clears the entry and reloads the tab. Keep it minimal.
payload.title: Clear/re-run dark detection when site mode changes; popup re-check action
